QF.id: XIII Introduction to Forwards and Futures_4

question.text: How does the cost-of-carry model for commodity futures differ from equity futures?

answer.text: Commodity futures include storage costs and may include a convenience yield: F = S + Storage costs - Convenience yield + Interest cost
Commodity futures use the same formula as equity futures with no modifications
Commodity futures are always priced at exactly the spot price
Commodity futures pricing ignores storage costs and only considers interest rates

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Commodities have physical storage costs (warehousing, insurance) that increase the carry cost. They also may have a 'convenience yield' — the benefit of holding the physical commodity (e.g., for production). So F = S(1+r+s-c)t where s = storage cost and c = convenience yield. **The Trap:** Equity futures don't have storage costs (shares are held in demat). Commodities don't have dividends. The convenience yield is unique to commodities. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series XVI Ch3 covers commodity cost-of-carry. Series VIII Ch3 for equity. Series XIII compares pricing models across asset classes. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** When convenience yield exceeds carry cost, commodity futures trade below spot (backwardation) — this is a signal of supply tightness in the physical market.
